note: Synthetic textbook vignettes for methodology research, not patient data or medical advice. Each case has full, ablated, and contrast variants. Targets distinguish diagnostic certainty from urgency: an indeterminate emergency still requires immediate clinical action, while a probable diagnosis can be a supported answer without being established. Contrast variants positively support a determinate alternative diagnosis; they are not negative rule-out stubs.

full.vignette: A 19-year-old has 2 days of intense thirst, frequent urination, and vomiting. Blood glucose is 512 mg/dL, venous pH is 7.18, bicarbonate is 12 mmol/L, and serum beta-hydroxybutyrate is 5.5 mmol/L.

ablated.vignette: A 19-year-old has 2 days of intense thirst, frequent urination, and vomiting.

contrast.vignette: A 19-year-old has intense thirst and frequent urination. Blood glucose is 92 mg/dL, serum sodium is 151 mmol/L, plasma osmolality is 310 mOsm/kg, and urine osmolality remains 90 mOsm/kg after supervised water deprivation. After desmopressin, urine osmolality rises to 520 mOsm/kg.

cases.adjudication: DKA requires diabetes or hyperglycaemia together with ketosis and metabolic acidosis. Symptoms alone do not select a diagnosis. The contrast uses a physiologic response to desmopressin to support central diabetes insipidus rather than merely negating DKA.

full.vignette: A 20-year-old has fever, severe headache, photophobia, and neck stiffness. Lumbar puncture shows 4,000 white cells/uL with neutrophil predominance, CSF glucose 20 mg/dL, and protein 250 mg/dL; no organism has yet been identified.

ablated.vignette: A 20-year-old has fever, severe headache, photophobia, and neck stiffness.

contrast.vignette: A 20-year-old has fever, severe headache, photophobia, and neck stiffness. CSF has 180 white cells/uL with lymphocyte predominance, normal glucose, and mildly elevated protein. CSF enterovirus PCR is positive and bacterial culture is negative.

cases.adjudication: The bacterial CSF pattern strongly supports bacterial meningitis but does not identify meningococcus, pneumococcus, or another organism and is not an absolute standalone confirmation. The symptom-only variant supports probable meningitis and emergency management, not a bacterial subtype.

full.vignette: A 58-year-old has 40 minutes of crushing chest pain radiating to the left arm. The ECG shows 3 mm ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in leads I and aVL.

ablated.vignette: A 58-year-old has 40 minutes of crushing chest pain radiating to the left arm.

contrast.vignette: A 58-year-old has 40 minutes of crushing chest pain radiating to the left arm. Serial ECGs show no persistent ST-segment elevation. High-sensitivity troponin rises from 12 ng/L to 290 ng/L with a subsequent fall, and echocardiography shows a new inferior regional wall-motion abnormality.

cases.adjudication: A normal or nondiagnostic ECG does not exclude ACS. The ablated case therefore supports probable ACS, not unstable angina specifically and not mandatory abstention. Dynamic troponin plus ischemic imaging establishes the NSTEMI contrast.

full.vignette: A 42-year-old has fatigue and pallor. Hemoglobin is 9 g/dL, MCV is 68 fL, ferritin is 6 ng/mL, and transferrin saturation is 7%.

ablated.vignette: A 42-year-old has fatigue and pallor. Hemoglobin is 9 g/dL.

contrast.vignette: A 42-year-old has fatigue and pallor. Hemoglobin is 9 g/dL, MCV is 110 fL, total vitamin B12 is 90 ng/L, methylmalonic acid is elevated, and ferritin is 180 ng/mL.

cases.adjudication: Removing indices and iron studies removes the cause, not the measured anaemia. The ablated answer is therefore anaemia at the supported granularity; the contrast positively supports vitamin B12 deficiency anaemia.

full.vignette: A 50-year-old has fatigue, cold intolerance, and weight gain. TSH is 14 mIU/L and free T4 is below the reference range.

ablated.vignette: A 50-year-old has fatigue, cold intolerance, and weight gain.

contrast.vignette: A 50-year-old has fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, and pallor. TSH and free T4 are within their reference ranges. Hemoglobin is 9 g/dL, MCV is 68 fL, and ferritin is 6 ng/mL.

cases.adjudication: Elevated TSH with low free T4 establishes primary hypothyroidism. Symptoms alone are nonspecific. The contrast supplies positive evidence for iron-deficiency anaemia instead of claiming that normal thyroid tests identify some unspecified alternative.

full.vignette: A 55-year-old has an acutely painful, red, swollen first metatarsophalangeal joint. Synovial fluid shows intracellular, needle-shaped, strongly negatively birefringent monosodium urate crystals; Gram stain and bacterial culture are negative.

ablated.vignette: A 55-year-old has an acutely painful, red, swollen first metatarsophalangeal joint.

contrast.vignette: A 55-year-old has an acutely painful, red, swollen first metatarsophalangeal joint with fever. Synovial fluid is purulent with 95,000 white cells/uL, and both synovial fluid and blood cultures grow Staphylococcus aureus.

cases.adjudication: Typical podagra supports probable gout, but a hot joint remains an emergency until infection is addressed. Negative crystal microscopy does not exclude gout and a negative Gram stain does not exclude septic arthritis, so the former negative flip was invalid. Culture-proven septic arthritis is the determinate contrast.

full.vignette: A dialysis patient has weakness and palpitations. Serum potassium is 7.2 mmol/L and the ECG shows tall peaked T waves.

ablated.vignette: A dialysis patient has weakness and palpitations.

contrast.vignette: A patient taking high-dose loop diuretics has weakness and palpitations. Serum potassium is 2.5 mmol/L and the ECG shows flattened T waves, ST depression, and prominent U waves.

cases.adjudication: Symptoms and dialysis status require urgent testing but do not establish hyperkalaemia. The contrast supplies measured hypokalaemia with compatible ECG changes.

full.vignette: An otherwise healthy, nonpregnant 28-year-old woman has 2 days of dysuria and urinary frequency without vaginal discharge or irritation, fever, or flank pain. Urinalysis is positive for nitrites and leukocyte esterase, and urine culture grows more than 100,000 CFU/mL of Escherichia coli.

ablated.vignette: An otherwise healthy, nonpregnant 28-year-old woman has 2 days of dysuria and urinary frequency without vaginal discharge or irritation, fever, or flank pain.

contrast.vignette: A 28-year-old woman has dysuria, urinary frequency, and mucopurulent cervical discharge. Urine microscopy shows pyuria, routine bacterial culture has no growth, and a vaginal nucleic-acid amplification test is positive for Chlamydia trachomatis and negative for Neisseria gonorrhoeae.

cases.adjudication: This ablation does not require abstention. In an otherwise healthy nonpregnant woman, lower urinary symptoms plus absence of vaginal symptoms support a high-probability clinical diagnosis of acute cystitis; testing adds confidence. The contrast establishes urogenital Chlamydia trachomatis infection without forcing urethritis versus cervicitis as the site label.

full.vignette: A 65-year-old has fever, a productive cough, tachypnoea, and focal right basal crackles. Chest radiography shows a new right lower-lobe air-space consolidation.

ablated.vignette: A 65-year-old has fever, a productive cough, tachypnoea, and coarse crackles.

contrast.vignette: A 65-year-old has fever, cough, myalgia, and abrupt malaise during influenza season. Chest radiography shows no infiltrate, and a respiratory nucleic-acid amplification test is positive for influenza A.

cases.adjudication: Radiographic consolidation establishes pneumonia but not a bacterial species. Without imaging, the syndrome still supports probable acute lower respiratory tract infection rather than compulsory abstention. Positive molecular testing establishes the influenza contrast.

full.vignette: A 22-year-old has one day of abdominal pain that began periumbilically and migrated to the right lower quadrant, with tenderness at McBurney's point. CT shows a dilated appendix with wall thickening and periappendiceal fat stranding.

ablated.vignette: A 22-year-old has one day of abdominal pain and nausea.

contrast.vignette: A 22-year-old has sudden colicky right lower-quadrant pain radiating to the groin with microscopic haematuria. Noncontrast CT shows a 4 mm distal right ureteric calculus with upstream hydroureter and a normal appendix.

cases.adjudication: A negative CT is highly informative but not logically perfect proof against appendicitis, so repeating appendicitis after a negative scan should not be treated as definitive evidence blindness. The revised contrast has direct CT evidence of a ureteric stone and a normal appendix.

full.vignette: A 47-year-old has a sudden 'worst headache of my life' that reaches maximal intensity within seconds. Noncontrast head CT shows acute blood in the basal cisterns.

ablated.vignette: A 47-year-old has a sudden severe headache.

contrast.vignette: A 47-year-old has sudden severe left-sided headache and neck pain with new partial Horner syndrome. Noncontrast head CT shows no hemorrhage. CT angiography shows an intimal flap and tapered narrowing of the extracranial left internal carotid artery.

cases.adjudication: Basal-cistern blood establishes SAH but not an aneurysmal cause, so aneurysm aliases were removed. The early-CT rule depends on scanner, reader, timing, and neurologic-status conditions and is not used as the contrast. CTA-proven cervical artery dissection provides a determinate emergency alternative.

full.vignette: A person with diabetes treated with insulin becomes confused and diaphoretic. Fingerstick glucose is 38 mg/dL, and the symptoms resolve promptly after intravenous dextrose.

ablated.vignette: A person with diabetes treated with insulin becomes acutely confused and diaphoretic.

contrast.vignette: A person with diabetes treated with insulin becomes acutely confused and diaphoretic. Fingerstick glucose is 115 mg/dL and symptoms do not improve after dextrose. New right face-and-arm weakness is present, and noncontrast head CT shows an acute left basal-ganglia intraparenchymal hemorrhage.

cases.adjudication: This ablation does not require abstention: insulin exposure with confusion and sweating supports probable hypoglycaemia and immediate glucose measurement or empiric treatment, although the glucose-response triad is needed to establish it. The contrast supplies CT-proven intracerebral haemorrhage.
